claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 12 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 12 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 12 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to ask you some questions about your child (or children).

**First question: How old is your child?**

The eligibility rules differ based on age:
- **Under 16**: Typically automatically qualify
- **16 to 19**: May qualify if in approved education or training
- **20 or over**: Generally not eligible

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have multiple children, let me know about all of them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit and would like to know if I'm eligible. I have one child - Alex, who's 12 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information! Let me assess the eligibility for your child Alex, who is 12 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news! Since Alex is 12 years old (under 16), they generally qualify age-wise. Now I need to check a few more criteria.

**Next question: Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

At 12 years old, it's unusual for children to be in paid work, but I need to confirm this for the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all. They're just in school like any normal 12-year-old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's what I expected. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex receiving any benefits in their own right?**

I'm asking specifically about benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit that would be claimed in Alex's own name (not yours). This would be unusual for a 12-year-old, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in their own name. They're just a regular kid.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Let me continue the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship?**

Again, this would be very unusual for a 12-year-old who's in regular school, but I need to check this as part of the eligibility criteria.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, of course not! Alex is only 12 and in school. They're nowhere near starting an apprenticeship.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely understood! Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now we need to check your residency status as the claimant.

**Question: Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **ANY** of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources (not claiming certain benefits)
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do any of these apply to you?** The most common situation is simply that you normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I live in Manchester with Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question: Are you responsible for Alex?**

You're considered responsible if **ANY** of the following applies:

1. **You live with Alex** (which you've already mentioned you do)
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep (pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit for their care - food, clothes, housing, etc.)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Since you've already told me you live in Manchester with Alex, this means you meet the responsibility requirement. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct. Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important question: Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another family member
- A former partner

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time. If someone else is claiming, they would need to agree to transfer the claim to you.

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question: What is Alex's current living situation?**

I need to check if any special circumstances apply:

- Is Alex currently **in hospital**?
- Is Alex **in local authority care**?
- Is Alex **fostered**?
- Or is Alex **living normally at home with you**?

Based on what you've told me, it sounds like Alex is living normally at home with you in Manchester. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. Alex just lives at home with me - nothing complicated like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final question: Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, are you receiving any payments from the local council specifically for Alex's care, accommodation, or living costs?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards Alex. I'm just a regular parent looking after my own child.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
# 🎉 Great News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

**Result: ELIGIBLE**

You meet all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements for Alex. Based on our assessment:

✅ Alex is 12 years old (under 16 - automatically qualifies)  
✅ Alex is not in paid work  
✅ Alex doesn't receive benefits in their own right  
✅ Alex is not in an apprenticeship  
✅ You live in the UK (Manchester)  
✅ You have parental responsibility and live with Alex  
✅ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex  
✅ Alex lives at home normally  
✅ The local council is not paying towards Alex's care  

## Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware**: If you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge (but you can still claim)
5. **Payments**: Typically made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

## How to Apply:

Visit: [https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

If you have any questions about the application process or the High Income Child Benefit Charge, feel free to ask!

reasonings: Alex is 12 years old (under 16, so automatically qualifies age-wise). Alex is not in paid work, does not receive any benefits in their own right, and is not in an apprenticeship. The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester) and lives with Alex, establishing responsibility. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex lives at home normally, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all UK Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 12-year-old child Alex. They are a UK resident with parental responsibility, living with the child, and no disqualifying factors apply.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 12 years old (under 16, so automatically qualifies age-wise). Alex is not in paid work, does not receive any benefits in their own right, and is not in an apprenticeship. The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester) and lives with Alex, establishing responsibility. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex lives at home normally, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all UK Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 12-year-old child Alex. They are a UK resident with parental responsibility, living with the child, and no disqualifying factors apply.
